parentQuestion: The primary visual cortex contains a map of visual space.

scaffolds.q: Visual information from the eyes is processed in the occipital lobe.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The occipital lobe, at the back of the brain, contains the primary visual cortex (V1).

scaffolds.q: The primary visual cortex is also called V1 or the calcarine cortex.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! V1 sits in the calcarine sulcus of the occipital lobe.

scaffolds.q: Visual information travels: retina → optic nerve → LGN (thalamus) → V1.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN) is the thalamic relay for vision.

scaffolds.q: Neighboring retinal cells project to neighboring cortical cells in V1.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! This preserves spatial relationships - called 'retinotopy' or topographic mapping.

scaffolds.q: The fovea (center of gaze) is overrepresented in V1 - 'cortical magnification.'
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The fovea covers ~1% of retina but takes ~50% of V1! High-resolution central vision.

scaffolds.q: Damage to a patch of V1 causes blindness in a corresponding patch of visual field.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The map IS the function. Destroy part of the map, that region of visual space goes dark.

scaffolds.q: The term for this spatial mapping is 'retinotopy' - the retina is mapped onto cortex.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Retino- (retina) + -topy (place). Spatial relationships are preserved across relay stations.

scaffolds.q: V1 contains a retinotopic map - a spatial representation of the visual field.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Neighboring points in visual space map to neighboring points in V1, with the fovea massively overrepresented.

scaffolds.q: The answer is TRUE - the primary visual cortex contains a map of visual space.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! V1 contains a retinotopic map - spatial relationships preserved from retina through LGN to cortex, with cortical magnification favoring central vision.
